players.name: Hernán Galíndez
players.bio: Born in Rosario but proudly Ecuadorian, Galíndez naturalised and became La Tri's first-choice shot-stopper. The 39-year-old veteran brings calm authority and big-game experience to what could be his World Cup swansong.
players.facts: Galíndez was born in Argentina — same city as Messi. The comparisons probably end there, but still a fun fact.

players.name: Félix Torres
players.bio: A powerful, no-nonsense centre-back who reads the game well beyond his years. Torres has been a consistent performer for La Tri and brings Brazilian club football experience from Internacional to Ecuador's defensive line.
players.facts: Torres has scored 5 international goals — impressive for a centre-back with just 49 caps. Defenders who chip in count too.

players.name: Piero Hincapié
players.bio: One of Europe's most exciting young defenders, Hincapié joined Arsenal after impressing at Bayer Leverkusen. Composed, quick, and technically gifted, the Esmeraldas-born 24-year-old is already a cornerstone of Ecuador's defence.
players.facts: Hincapié was part of Leverkusen's historic unbeaten Bundesliga title season in 2023-24 before moving to Arsenal.

players.name: Joel Ordóñez
players.bio: A teenage sensation who earned his stripes in Belgium's competitive Pro League with Club Brugge. Ordóñez is strong in the air, composed on the ball, and at just 22 represents Ecuador's defensive future — arriving at his first World Cup.
players.facts: Ordóñez was born in 2004 — meaning he could be playing a World Cup before he turns 22. Football moves fast.

players.name: Jordy Alcívar
players.bio: A hard-working defensive midfielder who combines graft with tactical discipline. Alcívar returned to Independiente del Valle after European stints and brings energy and tenacity to Ecuador's engine room at his first World Cup.
players.facts: Alcívar plays for the club that pioneered Ecuador's youth football revolution — Independiente del Valle's academy is legendary.

players.name: Willian Pacho
players.bio: Pacho swapped Royal Antwerp for Paris Saint-Germain in 2024, confirming his status as one of world football's finest young centre-backs. Aggressive, dominant in the air, and born in tiny Quinindé — his rise has been extraordinary.
players.facts: Pacho moved to PSG for a reported €40m fee — not bad for a kid from a small Ecuadorian town of 40,000 people.

players.name: Pervis Estupiñán
players.bio: One of the most dynamic left-backs in world football, Estupiñán moved to AC Milan after dazzling at Brighton. An overlapping, marauding full-back with an eye for goal, he is the engine on Ecuador's left flank.
players.facts: Estupiñán was so impressive at Brighton that they reportedly turned down bids before finally selling him to Milan. Worth the wait.

players.name: Anthony Valencia
players.bio: Son of Ecuadorian legend Antonio Valencia, Anthony is carving his own path. The young winger has pace to burn and is already contributing for the national team, arriving at this World Cup with just 3 caps but enormous potential.
players.facts: Anthony Valencia shares more than a surname with his father — he also inherited the right flank. Legacy is real.

players.name: John Yeboah
players.bio: Born in Hamburg to Ghanaian parents, Yeboah chose Ecuador and has been a lively, direct presence in attack. His pace and trickery make him a constant handful, and he arrives at this World Cup with growing international experience.
players.facts: Yeboah was born in Germany, has a Ghanaian name, and plays for Ecuador. International football is beautifully complicated.

players.name: Kendry Páez
players.bio: The crown jewel. Born in 2007, Páez is one of the most exciting teenagers on the planet. Technically brilliant with vision beyond his years, the number 10 carries Ecuador's creative hopes — and he's still a teenager at this World Cup.
players.facts: Páez signed a pre-contract with Chelsea aged 16 before joining River Plate. His future is already the talk of world football.

players.name: Kevin Rodríguez
players.bio: Born in Colombia but raised Ecuadorian, Rodríguez is a dynamic, versatile attacker with an eye for goal. He has become a reliable presence for La Tri, combining tireless pressing with creative play on the flanks.
players.facts: Rodríguez was born in Colombia but represents Ecuador — his current bio in the system was accidentally about Irish etymology. Oops.

players.name: Moisés Ramírez
players.bio: Ramírez provides experienced backup between the sticks despite playing his club football in Greece with Kifisia. The Guayaquil-born keeper has earned his seven caps through solid domestic form and reliable shot-stopping.
players.facts: Ramírez plays club football in Greece — making him one of the more geographically adventurous keepers at this World Cup.

players.name: Enner Valencia
players.bio: Ecuador's all-time top scorer and national icon. With 49 goals in 105 caps, Valencia has carried La Tri for over a decade. At 36, this World Cup may be his farewell — and what a career it has been for the legend from San Lorenzo.
players.facts: Valencia scored Ecuador's first three goals at the 2014 World Cup. He basically is Ecuadorian football in human form.

players.name: Alan Minda
players.bio: A rapid, direct winger from Esmeraldas who joined Atlético Mineiro after impressing in Europe. Minda's electric pace and technical ability on the right make him a constant threat and a bright young spark in Ecuador's attack.
players.facts: Minda is one of three Ecuador players born in Esmeraldas at this tournament — the coastal city is clearly a football hotbed.

players.name: Pedro Vite
players.bio: A creative, technically adept attacking midfielder who has moved to Mexican giants Pumas. Vite offers flair and unpredictability in the final third, providing Ecuador with an inventive option from midfield.
players.facts: Vite plays in Mexico's Liga MX — one of the host nations' leagues. He'll know exactly what kind of welcome to expect.

players.name: Jordy Caicedo
players.bio: A powerful, physical centre-forward who has plied his trade in Argentina with Huracán. Caicedo offers a different dimension to Ecuador's attack — strong in the air and a reliable finisher with four goals in 20 caps.
players.facts: Caicedo and Galíndez are teammates at Huracán in Argentina — two Ecuador internationals at the same Argentine club.

players.name: Ángelo Preciado
players.bio: An energetic, attack-minded right-back with over 55 caps, Preciado has been a La Tri regular for years. His tireless running and ability to deliver from wide make him a key contributor to Ecuador's dynamic full-back play.
players.facts: Preciado has 55 caps and zero international goals — remarkable consistency for a man who loves getting forward. So close.

players.name: Denil Castillo
players.bio: A young, combative central midfielder from the Eloy Alfaro Canton with just five caps to his name. Castillo has earned his place at this World Cup through impressive performances for Midtjylland in Denmark's Superliga.
players.facts: Castillo plays in Denmark, born in Ecuador, competing in a World Cup in North America. Geography is just a suggestion.

players.name: Gonzalo Plata
players.bio: A skilful, pacey winger who has lit up multiple leagues across Europe and South America. Now at Flamengo, Plata's direct running, dribbling ability and eight international goals make him one of Ecuador's most dangerous wide threats.
players.facts: Plata has played in Spain, Qatar, and Brazil during his career. He's seen more of the world than most travel bloggers.

players.name: Nilson Angulo
players.bio: A lively left-winger who swapped South American football for the English Championship with Sunderland. Angulo's pace and directness on the flank offer Ecuador an exciting option wide left, with two goals in 14 caps already.
players.facts: Angulo plays at the Stadium of Light in Sunderland — quite a contrast to the Ecuadorian sunshine he grew up in.

players.name: Alan Franco
players.bio: One of Ecuador's most experienced outfield players with 58 caps, Franco is a dependable, box-to-box midfielder who has thrived at Atlético Mineiro. His experience and leadership are invaluable to La Tri's midfield structure.
players.facts: Franco was born in the wonderfully named Alfredo Baquerizo Moreno, Ecuador — try fitting that on a shirt.

players.name: Gonzalo Valle
players.bio: Third-choice keeper Valle is a proven performer in Ecuador's domestic game with LDU Quito, one of the country's biggest clubs. The Riobamba-born stopper provides experienced cover and brings LDU's winning culture to the squad.
players.facts: Valle plays for LDU Quito, whose altitude of 2,850m means their home games are basically played in the clouds.

players.name: Moisés Caicedo
players.bio: Chelsea's midfield metronome and Ecuador's heartbeat. Caicedo is one of the world's best defensive midfielders — relentless, powerful, and technically excellent. At just 24 with 61 caps, his ceiling remains spectacularly high.
players.facts: Chelsea paid a British record £115m for Caicedo in 2023. Ecuador produced him for free. Best deal in Ecuadorian history.

players.name: Jeremy Arévalo
players.bio: Born in Santander, Spain, Arévalo is one of football's most intriguing young prospects. The Stuttgart striker chose Ecuador and arrives at this World Cup with just 4 caps — a genuine wildcard with European club pedigree.
players.facts: Arévalo was born in Spain, develops in Germany, and plays for Ecuador. The modern footballer defies all borders.

players.name: Jackson Porozo
players.bio: A sturdy, reliable centre-back from San Lorenzo — the same town as Enner Valencia. Porozo brings physical presence and composure to Ecuador's defensive options, with experience gained in Mexico's competitive Liga MX with Tijuana.
players.facts: Porozo and Enner Valencia both hail from San Lorenzo, Ecuador. That small town is punching well above its weight.

players.name: Yaimar Medina
players.bio: A young, attack-minded left-back from Eloy Alfaro who has developed impressively at Genk in Belgium. Medina is one of Ecuador's brightest defensive prospects, combining defensive solidity with the ambition to get forward.
players.facts: Medina was born in 2004, plays in Belgium, and is at his first World Cup aged 21. The journey has only just begun.
